painPoints: Making subtle mistakes that signal 'non-native speaker'
Not knowing which mistakes matter most

conversion.nextStep: Book a free strategy call to identify your specific mistake patterns

seo.metaDescription: Fix the 10 most common English mistakes that immediately signal 'non-native speaker' to North American clients. Includes fixes you can apply today.

en.title: The 10 Most Common Mistakes in Executive English
en.subtitle: Subtle Errors That Undermine Your Credibility (And How to Fix Them)
en.description: These 10 mistakes are subtle but they immediately signal 'non-native speaker' to North American clients. Fix these and you'll sound more polished instantly.
en.headline: Stop Making These Mistakes That Cost You Credibility
en.valueProposition: Identify and fix the 10 most common errors that Latin American professionals make—errors that native speakers notice but rarely correct.

leadCapture.formSubheadline: Fixes you can apply in your next meeting
leadCapture.successMessage: Check your inbox! Your guide is on its way.

sections.title: Why These Mistakes Matter
sections.content: These errors are subtle but they immediately signal 'non-native speaker' to North American clients. The problem? Native speakers rarely correct you. They understand what you mean, so they let it slide. But they notice. And in high-stakes business situations, these small errors accumulate into an impression of lower fluency than you actually have.

items.problem: In English, 'actually' implies correction or surprise. Using it habitually sounds condescending.
items.fix: Delete 'actually' from most sentences. Reserve it for genuine corrections.
items.wrongExample: Actually, I think we should consider...
items.rightExample: I think we should consider...

items.problem: Excessive hedging undermines your authority. Executives state positions; they don't constantly qualify.
items.fix: State your position directly. Save 'I think' for opinions, not facts.
items.wrongExample: I think the deadline is next Friday. I think we need more resources.
items.rightExample: The deadline is next Friday. We need more resources.

items.problem: This is a direct translation from Spanish. In English, it sounds informal and uncertain.
items.fix: Use 'right?' or 'don't you think?' for tag questions, or eliminate them entirely.
items.wrongExample: We should move forward, no?
items.rightExample: We should move forward, right?

items.problem: 'Doubt' in English means skepticism or disbelief. You mean 'question.'
items.fix: Replace 'I have a doubt' with 'I have a question' or 'I'm unclear on...'
items.wrongExample: I have a doubt about the timeline.
items.rightExample: I have a question about the timeline.

items.problem: Starting with 'Sorry, but...' or 'Sorry to bother you' undermines your authority.
items.fix: Replace apologies with direct statements or gratitude.
items.wrongExample: Sorry, but I disagree with that approach.
items.rightExample: I see it differently. Here's why...

items.problem: 'Assist' sounds formal and stiff. Native speakers say 'help.'
items.fix: Use 'help' in almost all cases. It's more natural and direct.
items.wrongExample: I can assist you with that project.
items.rightExample: I can help you with that project.

items.problem: This is a literal translation. English uses 'right now' or 'currently.'
items.fix: Replace 'in this moment' with 'right now,' 'currently,' or 'at the moment.'
items.wrongExample: In this moment, we're focused on Q4.
items.rightExample: Right now, we're focused on Q4.

items.problem: Excessive use of 'very' weakens your statements instead of strengthening them.
items.fix: Use stronger verbs and adjectives instead of 'very + weak word.'
items.wrongExample: This is a very important project that's very urgent.
items.rightExample: This is a critical project that's urgent.

items.problem: Missing the object. English requires 'I will send you THE EMAIL/DOCUMENT.'
items.fix: Always include what you're sending: the file, the email, the document.
items.wrongExample: I will send you by end of day.
items.rightExample: I'll send you the proposal by end of day.

items.problem: This is a direct translation from Spanish. English uses 'What do you call...?'
items.fix: Replace 'How do you call' with 'What do you call' or 'What's the word for...'
items.wrongExample: How do you call this in English?
items.rightExample: What do you call this in English?

es.subtitle: Errores Sutiles Que Socavan Tu Credibilidad (Y Cómo Corregirlos)
es.description: Estos 10 errores son sutiles pero inmediatamente señalan 'hablante no nativo' a clientes norteamericanos. Corrígelos y sonarás más pulido al instante.

sections.title: Por Qué Estos Errores Importan
sections.content: Estos errores son sutiles pero inmediatamente señalan 'hablante no nativo' a clientes norteamericanos. ¿El problema? Los nativos rara vez te corrigen. Entienden lo que quieres decir, así que lo dejan pasar. Pero lo notan. Y en situaciones de negocios de alto nivel, estos pequeños errores se acumulan en una impresión de menor fluidez de la que realmente tienes.

items.problem: En inglés, 'actually' implica corrección o sorpresa. Usarlo habitualmente suena condescendiente.
items.fix: Elimina 'actually' de la mayoría de las oraciones. Resérvalo para correcciones genuinas.
items.wrongExample: Actually, I think we should consider...
items.rightExample: I think we should consider...

items.problem: El exceso de cobertura socava tu autoridad. Los ejecutivos declaran posiciones; no califican constantemente.
items.fix: Declara tu posición directamente. Guarda 'I think' para opiniones, no hechos.
items.wrongExample: I think the deadline is next Friday. I think we need more resources.
items.rightExample: The deadline is next Friday. We need more resources.

items.problem: Esta es una traducción directa del español. En inglés, suena informal e inseguro.
items.fix: Usa 'right?' o 'don't you think?' para preguntas de confirmación, o elimínalas por completo.
items.wrongExample: We should move forward, no?
items.rightExample: We should move forward, right?

items.problem: 'Doubt' en inglés significa escepticismo o incredulidad. Quieres decir 'question.'
items.fix: Reemplaza 'I have a doubt' con 'I have a question' o 'I'm unclear on...'
items.wrongExample: I have a doubt about the timeline.
items.rightExample: I have a question about the timeline.

items.problem: Comenzar con 'Sorry, but...' o 'Sorry to bother you' socava tu autoridad.
items.fix: Reemplaza disculpas con declaraciones directas o gratitud.
items.wrongExample: Sorry, but I disagree with that approach.
items.rightExample: I see it differently. Here's why...

items.problem: 'Assist' suena formal y rígido. Los nativos dicen 'help.'
items.fix: Usa 'help' en casi todos los casos. Es más natural y directo.
items.wrongExample: I can assist you with that project.
items.rightExample: I can help you with that project.

items.problem: Esta es una traducción literal. El inglés usa 'right now' o 'currently.'
items.fix: Reemplaza 'in this moment' con 'right now,' 'currently,' o 'at the moment.'
items.wrongExample: In this moment, we're focused on Q4.
items.rightExample: Right now, we're focused on Q4.

items.problem: El uso excesivo de 'very' debilita tus declaraciones en lugar de fortalecerlas.
items.fix: Usa verbos y adjetivos más fuertes en lugar de 'very + palabra débil.'
items.wrongExample: This is a very important project that's very urgent.
items.rightExample: This is a critical project that's urgent.

items.problem: Falta el objeto. El inglés requiere 'I will send you THE EMAIL/DOCUMENT.'
items.fix: Siempre incluye qué estás enviando: el archivo, el email, el documento.
items.wrongExample: I will send you by end of day.
items.rightExample: I'll send you the proposal by end of day.

items.problem: Esta es una traducción directa del español. El inglés usa 'What do you call...?'
items.fix: Reemplaza 'How do you call' con 'What do you call' o 'What's the word for...'
items.wrongExample: How do you call this in English?
items.rightExample: What do you call this in English?
